questions.prompt: The difference between the maximum and minimum of a data set.
questions.explanation: The range is the difference between the maximum and minimum values—a simple measure of data spread. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.3 tests using range to compare two populations. Worked example: scores 70, 75, 80, 85, 90: range = 90 − 70 = 20. State-test items test range computation and interpretation as a measure of variability: small range = data clustered; large range = data spread out. Range is sensitive to outliers; a single extreme value can inflate the range. Interquartile range (IQR) is a more robust measure but typically taught in Grade 8 onward. For Grade 7 comparative inference, range gives a quick sense of variability between two groups.

questions.prompt: Comparing two populations using their measures of center and spread.
questions.explanation: Comparative inference uses measures of center (mean, median) and spread (range) to compare two populations or samples. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.4 explicitly tests using 'measures of center and measures of variability for numerical data from random samples to draw informal comparative inferences about two populations.' Worked example: School A scores have mean 85, range 30; School B scores have mean 78, range 20. Inferences: School A is higher on average but more variable; School B is lower but more consistent. State-test items test multi-step comparative inference with quantitative reasoning. The state rubric awards points for both correct statistical computation AND reasonable interpretation of what the statistics imply about the populations.

questions.prompt: A chart showing data with horizontal or vertical bars.
questions.explanation: A bar graph displays data using rectangular bars of length proportional to the values they represent—best for categorical data (favorite color, type of pet). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP relies on data displays for comparative inference. Worked example: a bar graph showing 30 students preferring pizza, 20 burgers, 15 salads. State-test items test bar-graph reading and creation; bars can be vertical or horizontal, but lengths must be proportional to the data values. Distinguish bar graphs (categorical data, separate bars with gaps) from histograms (numerical data, touching bars showing frequency distribution). Bar graphs answer 'how many?' questions about categories.

questions.prompt: A chart using small symbols to represent data values.
questions.explanation: A dot plot (line plot) displays numerical data with dots stacked above a number line. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.3 tests data-display interpretation including dot plots. Worked example: a dot plot of test scores 75, 80, 80, 85, 85, 85, 90 shows: 75 (1 dot), 80 (2 dots), 85 (3 dots), 90 (1 dot). Dot plots show distribution (where data clusters), outliers, and mode visually. State-test items test dot-plot interpretation: identifying mode, median, range; comparing distributions of two groups stacked on the same axis. Dot plots are ideal for small data sets (10-30 values); for larger sets, histograms are more efficient.

questions.prompt: A chart showing the distribution of numerical data in intervals.
questions.explanation: A histogram displays the distribution of numerical data using bars representing frequencies of values within intervals (bins). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.3 tests distribution analysis using histograms. Worked example: a histogram of student heights might use 5-cm intervals (140-145, 145-150, etc.) with bar heights showing how many students fall in each range. Unlike bar graphs, histogram bars touch (no gaps) because intervals are continuous. State-test items test histogram reading, interpretation of shape (symmetric, skewed, bimodal), and comparison of two histograms. Histograms reveal distribution shape, center, and spread better than single statistics; they are the standard display for continuous numerical data.

questions.prompt: A chart displaying the five-number summary of a data set.
questions.explanation: A box plot displays the five-number summary—minimum, Q1 (first quartile), median, Q3 (third quartile), maximum—as a box and whiskers. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.4 tests using box plots for comparative inference. Worked example: a box plot of test scores shows the median as a line inside the box, with the box covering 50% of the data (Q1 to Q3), and whiskers extending to min and max. State-test items test box-plot interpretation: identifying median, range, and IQR; comparing two box plots side-by-side to compare distributions. Box plots are excellent for comparing distributions because they show center, spread, and skew simultaneously in a compact display.

questions.prompt: A display that shows the shape and spread of two data sets side-by-side.
questions.explanation: A comparative display shows two or more data sets side-by-side on a common scale—parallel box plots, dual histograms, side-by-side dot plots—to enable direct comparison. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.4 explicitly tests using such displays for comparative inference. Worked example: side-by-side box plots of School A and School B test scores let you compare medians, ranges, and IQRs at a glance. State-test items test multi-step inference from comparative displays: 'Which school has higher median?' 'Which school has greater variability?' 'Which school has more students above 90?' The state rubric awards points for both correct readings of the displays AND reasonable comparative claims supported by the visual evidence.

questions.prompt: Using a sample to estimate a population parameter.
questions.explanation: Statistical inference is the process of using sample data to estimate or make claims about a population. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.1 and 7.SP.2 explicitly test using random samples to draw inferences about populations. Worked example: a random sample of 100 voters shows 55% favor candidate A; we infer that approximately 55% of all voters favor A, with some margin of error. State-test items test the inferential reasoning: 'Based on the sample, about how many of the 1,500 students would prefer pizza?' Answer: multiply sample proportion by population size: 0.55 × 1,500 ≈ 825. Inferences are always estimates with uncertainty—larger random samples produce more accurate inferences.

questions.prompt: Estimate of a value based on the sample.
questions.explanation: A sample-based estimate uses sample data to predict a population characteristic—the sample mean estimates the population mean, sample proportion estimates population proportion. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.2 explicitly tests sample-based estimation. Worked example: if 18 of 60 randomly sampled people are left-handed, the sample proportion is 0.30, so we estimate 30% of the population is left-handed. State-test items test sample-based estimation in proportional contexts: 'A random sample of 80 students shows 24 prefer green. How many of the 2,000 students would prefer green?' Setup: 24/80 = x/2000; cross-multiply 80x = 48,000; divide x = 600. Always show the proportional reasoning explicitly.

questions.prompt: How accurate a sample-based estimate is likely to be.
questions.explanation: Margin of error indicates how much a sample estimate may differ from the true population value—typically expressed as ±x%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.2 explicitly mentions to 'gauge how far off the estimate or prediction might be.' Worked example: if a sample estimates 55% favor candidate A with margin of error ±3%, the true value is likely between 52% and 58%. Smaller samples have larger margins; larger samples have smaller margins. State-test items test informal margin-of-error reasoning: 'The sample shows 60% favor pizza. The true value is probably somewhere between 55% and 65%, depending on sample size.' Formal margin-of-error calculation comes in high school statistics.

questions.prompt: The result expected from a fair process.
questions.explanation: Theoretical probability is the predicted likelihood of an event based on mathematical reasoning—favorable outcomes / total outcomes. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 tests probability and 7.SP.6 connects theoretical to experimental probability. Worked example: rolling a standard die, theoretical probability of rolling a 4 = 1/6 (1 favorable out of 6 possible). State-test items test theoretical probability for fair dice, coins, spinners, and card draws. Theoretical probability assumes all outcomes are equally likely (a fair process); when the process is biased, experimental probability provides a better estimate. Theoretical probability connects to Unit 6 (probability) and provides expected long-run frequencies.

questions.prompt: The probability found by conducting experiments.
questions.explanation: Experimental probability is the observed frequency of an event from actual trials: favorable outcomes / total trials. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 tests experimental probability through 'developing a probability model.' Worked example: rolling a die 60 times and getting 12 fours: experimental probability = 12/60 = 0.20 = 20%. Theoretical probability for a fair die is 1/6 ≈ 16.7%; the experimental value approaches the theoretical value as the number of trials increases (law of large numbers). State-test items test experimental probability calculation and comparison to theoretical predictions: 'After 1,000 trials, the experimental probability should be close to the theoretical value.' Connects directly to Unit 6 (probability).

questions.prompt: The likelihood of an event occurring, expressed from 0 to 1.
questions.explanation: Probability is the numerical measure of how likely an event is to occur, expressed as a value from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 explicitly tests understanding 'that the probability of a chance event is a number between 0 and 1 that expresses the likelihood of the event occurring.' Probability can be expressed as fraction (1/4), decimal (0.25), or percent (25%). Worked example: rolling a 3 on a fair die has probability 1/6 ≈ 0.167 ≈ 16.7%. State-test items test probability scale interpretation: 0 = impossible, 0.5 = equally likely as not, 1 = certain. All probabilities must satisfy 0 ≤ P ≤ 1; values outside this range indicate calculation error.

questions.prompt: An event that is certain to occur.
questions.explanation: An event with probability 1 (or 100%) is certain to occur. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 explicitly tests the probability scale, including certainty. Examples: rolling a number less than 7 on a standard die (P = 6/6 = 1); the sun rising tomorrow (essentially certain). Worked variations: P = 0 means impossible (rolling an 8 on a standard die); P = 0.5 means equally likely as not (flipping heads on a fair coin). State-test items test probability-scale reasoning: 'Which of the following has probability 1?' Answer: any event that includes all possible outcomes for the chance experiment. Certainty in probability is a theoretical extreme; real-world events rarely have exactly probability 1.

questions.prompt: An event that cannot occur.
questions.explanation: An event with probability 0 (or 0%) cannot occur—it is impossible. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 tests the probability scale. Examples: rolling a 7 on a standard 6-sided die (P = 0/6 = 0); drawing a blue ball from a bag containing only red and green balls. Worked variations: P = 0.1 means unlikely; P = 0.5 means equally likely as not; P = 0.9 means very likely; P = 1 means certain. State-test items test probability-scale reasoning: 'Which of the following has probability 0?' Answer: any event that has no favorable outcomes in the sample space. Impossible events combine with possible events through addition rules; P(A or impossible) = P(A).

questions.prompt: The probability of an event found by counting equally likely outcomes.
questions.explanation: Theoretical probability is calculated as favorable outcomes ÷ total possible outcomes, assuming all outcomes are equally likely. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.A explicitly tests using theoretical probability where 'all outcomes are equally likely.' Worked example: rolling a fair die, P(even number) = 3/6 = 1/2 (favorable: 2, 4, 6; total: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Theoretical probability for a fair coin: P(heads) = 1/2; for a standard deck: P(red card) = 26/52 = 1/2. State-test items test theoretical probability for fair dice, coins, spinners, and card draws; always identify favorable outcomes and total outcomes carefully. Theoretical probability assumes the underlying process is unbiased.

questions.prompt: The probability found by performing experiments and recording results.
questions.explanation: Experimental probability is calculated as favorable outcomes ÷ total trials from actual experiments. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.B explicitly tests 'develop a probability model by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.' Worked example: spinning a spinner 100 times and getting blue 30 times gives experimental probability P(blue) = 30/100 = 0.30. The law of large numbers states experimental probability approaches theoretical probability as trials increase; with infinite trials, they would be identical. State-test items test experimental vs. theoretical comparison: 'After many trials, the experimental probability should be close to the theoretical value. A large discrepancy suggests the process is biased or the trial count is too small.'

questions.prompt: P(heads) on a fair coin.
questions.explanation: On a fair coin, the two outcomes—heads and tails—are equally likely. P(heads) = favorable/total = 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 and 7.SP.7 test simple probability for fair processes. Worked variations: P(tails) = 1/2 (also 50%); P(heads or tails) = 1 (certain). For a coin flipped 100 times, expected number of heads = 100 × (1/2) = 50, though experimental results may differ due to randomness. State-test items test fair-coin probability extensively as the simplest probability example; results extend to compound events involving multiple coin flips, dice rolls, and spinners.

questions.prompt: P(rolling a 3) on a fair die.
questions.explanation: On a fair die, six outcomes—1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6—are equally likely. P(rolling a 3) = 1/6 ≈ 0.167 ≈ 16.7%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.A tests theoretical probability with equally likely outcomes. Worked variations: P(rolling a 4 or higher) = 3/6 = 1/2; P(rolling an even number) = 3/6 = 1/2; P(rolling less than 7) = 6/6 = 1 (certain); P(rolling 0) = 0/6 = 0 (impossible). State-test items test fair-die probability for various event compositions; always count favorable outcomes carefully and divide by 6 (the total outcomes on a standard die). Some problems involve non-standard dice (e.g., a 10-sided die), where total = 10.

questions.prompt: P(red) when drawing from 4 red, 6 blue, and 5 green marbles.
questions.explanation: To find probability: P(red) = number of red / total marbles. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 tests basic probability calculation. Worked example: 4 red + 6 blue + 5 green = 15 total marbles. P(red) = 4/15 ≈ 0.267 ≈ 26.7%. State-test items test probability with bag/marble/cup scenarios involving multiple colors. Variants: P(blue) = 6/15 = 2/5; P(green) = 5/15 = 1/3; P(red or blue) = 10/15 = 2/3 (add probabilities of mutually exclusive events). Always express probability as a simplified fraction or equivalent decimal/percent—the state rubric awards full credit for simplified forms like 2/5 over equivalent unsimplified forms like 6/15.

questions.prompt: P(not rolling a 3) on a fair die.
questions.explanation: Probability of complement: P(not A) = 1 − P(A). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 implicitly tests the complement rule. Worked example: P(rolling a 3) = 1/6, so P(not rolling a 3) = 1 − 1/6 = 5/6 ≈ 0.833. Alternative count: not 3 means rolling 1, 2, 4, 5, or 6 = 5 favorable outcomes, so P = 5/6. State-test items test complement reasoning explicitly: 'What is the probability of NOT [event]?' Answer: 1 − P(event). Complement is faster than counting all non-A outcomes when A has many possibilities (e.g., P(at least one head) is computed as 1 − P(no heads)—much faster than enumerating all 'at least one head' cases).

questions.prompt: P(an event) when 10 of 40 outcomes are favorable.
questions.explanation: P(event) = favorable / total = 10/40 = 1/4 = 0.25 = 25%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.A tests probability with equally likely outcomes; always simplify the resulting fraction. Worked example: a spinner divided into 40 equal sections with 10 marked 'win': P(win) = 10/40 = 1/4. State-test items test simplified-fraction probability reporting; 10/40, 5/20, 1/4, 0.25, 25% are all equivalent—the simplified form 1/4 is preferred for full credit. Practice: a bag of 50 candies with 15 red gives P(red) = 15/50 = 3/10 (simplified). Always check whether the problem specifies fraction, decimal, or percent form; match the requested form.

questions.prompt: Two events whose outcomes do not affect each other.
questions.explanation: Independent events have outcomes that do not affect each other—the probability of one event doesn't change based on the other's result. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8 explicitly tests compound events including independent events. Worked example: flipping a coin and rolling a die are independent—getting heads doesn't change the die's probability. P(heads AND rolling a 3) = P(heads) × P(rolling 3) = 1/2 × 1/6 = 1/12. State-test items test the multiplication rule for independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). Contrast with dependent events (drawing two cards without replacement), where the probability of the second depends on the first. Independence is the simpler case—and the most-tested in Grade 7.

questions.prompt: P(heads then heads) when flipping a fair coin twice.
questions.explanation: For two independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.A explicitly tests probability of compound events. Worked example: P(heads then heads) = P(heads) × P(heads) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4. State-test items test multi-step compound probability: P(heads then tails) = 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4; P(any 2-outcome sequence) = 1/4 for each. Tree diagrams illustrate compound events: starting from the root, branches split into possible outcomes, and the probability of a path equals the product of probabilities along it. Practice with three flips: P(HHH) = 1/2 × 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/8.

questions.prompt: P(rolling a 3 and then a 5) on two dice.
questions.explanation: For two independent dice: P(roll 3 then 5) = P(3) × P(5) = 1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.A tests compound probability with independent events. Worked variations: P(rolling two even numbers) = (1/2) × (1/2) = 1/4; P(rolling doubles) = sum of P(1,1) + P(2,2) + P(3,3) + P(4,4) + P(5,5) + P(6,6) = 6 × (1/36) = 6/36 = 1/6. State-test items test two-dice probability for specific outcomes, sums, and patterns. Total outcomes for two dice = 6 × 6 = 36; favorable outcomes / 36 gives probability. List or table of outcomes helps visualize compound probability for two dice.

questions.prompt: Two events that cannot both happen at the same time.
questions.explanation: Mutually exclusive events cannot both occur in a single trial; their intersection is empty. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8 contrasts mutually exclusive (P(A and B) = 0) with non-mutually-exclusive events. Worked example: rolling a die and getting a 3 OR a 5—these can't happen on a single roll. P(3 or 5) = P(3) + P(5) = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3. State-test items test the addition rule for mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). For non-mutually-exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B); the overlap is subtracted to avoid double-counting. Mutually exclusive events are the simpler case—most often tested in Grade 7.

questions.prompt: P(rolling a 3 or a 5) on a fair die.
questions.explanation: For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8 tests compound probability with mutually exclusive events. Worked example: P(rolling a 3 or 5) = P(3) + P(5) = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3 ≈ 0.333. Alternative: count favorable outcomes (3, 5) = 2; total = 6; P = 2/6 = 1/3. State-test items test the addition rule for mutually exclusive events: P(odd number) = P(1) + P(3) + P(5) = 3/6 = 1/2; P(less than 4) = P(1) + P(2) + P(3) = 3/6 = 1/2. Always check if events are mutually exclusive before adding; if they can overlap, subtract the overlap to avoid double-counting.

questions.prompt: A model assuming all outcomes are equally likely.
questions.explanation: A uniform probability model assigns equal probability to each outcome, used when the underlying process is fair: fair coins, dice, spinners with equal sections. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.A explicitly tests uniform models: 'develop a uniform probability model by assigning equal probability to all outcomes.' Worked example: rolling a fair die uses uniform model with P(each outcome) = 1/6. State-test items test uniform-model recognition and application; the model fails when outcomes are not equally likely (loaded die, biased spinner). Uniform models are the foundation for theoretical probability—P(event) = favorable/total assumes the uniform model.

questions.prompt: A model based on observed frequencies in experiments.
questions.explanation: An empirical probability model assigns probabilities based on observed frequencies from experiments, not assumed fairness. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.B explicitly tests this: 'develop a probability model by observing frequencies in data generated from a chance process.' Worked example: spinning a biased spinner 200 times and observing 90 reds gives empirical P(red) = 90/200 = 0.45. The empirical model can detect bias—if the theoretical model predicted P(red) = 1/3 but experimental P(red) = 0.45, the spinner is biased toward red. State-test items test empirical model construction and comparison to theoretical models; large discrepancies suggest the process is not fair.

questions.prompt: The list of all possible outcomes for a chance experiment.
questions.explanation: The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of a chance experiment. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.B explicitly tests sample space identification: 'represent sample spaces for compound events using methods such as organized lists, tables, and tree diagrams.' Worked example: flipping two coins gives sample space {HH, HT, TH, TT}—four equally likely outcomes; rolling two dice gives 36 outcomes (6 × 6 combinations). State-test items test sample-space construction for compound events: 'List all outcomes when flipping a coin and rolling a die: H1, H2, H3, H4, H5, H6, T1, T2, T3, T4, T5, T6—12 outcomes total.' Always count the sample space size first; favorable outcomes / sample space size = probability.

questions.prompt: A diagram showing all outcomes branching from each event.
questions.explanation: A tree diagram displays all outcomes of a sequence of events as branches splitting from a starting point, with probabilities labeled on each branch. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.B explicitly tests tree diagrams. Worked example: for two coin flips, the tree starts with one branch labeled 'H' (P = 1/2) and one labeled 'T' (P = 1/2); each then branches into 'H' and 'T' again, giving four end paths: HH, HT, TH, TT—each with probability 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4. State-test items test tree diagrams for two- and three-step events: the probability of a specific path equals the product of probabilities along it. Tree diagrams illustrate compound events visually and prevent miscounting outcomes.

questions.prompt: The probability of a sequence of independent events.
questions.explanation: For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.A explicitly tests the multiplication rule. Worked example: P(heads then rolling a 5) = P(heads) × P(rolling 5) = 1/2 × 1/6 = 1/12 ≈ 0.083. For three independent events: P(A and B and C) = P(A) × P(B) × P(C). State-test items test multi-step compound probability calculation: 'P(coin lands heads AND die shows 6) = 1/2 × 1/6 = 1/12'; 'P(drawing red then white then green from a bag, with replacement) = P(red) × P(white) × P(green) = product of individual probabilities.' Independence is essential for the multiplication rule; without it, conditional probability applies (Grade 8+).

questions.prompt: The expected number of times an event occurs in n trials.
questions.explanation: Expected value = probability × number of trials, predicting the long-run average number of times an event occurs. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 connects probability to predicted frequency. Worked example: flipping a coin 100 times, expected number of heads = 0.5 × 100 = 50. For a fair die rolled 60 times, expected 3s = (1/6) × 60 = 10. State-test items test expected value in real contexts: 'A 25% sale rate predicts 25 sales out of 100 customers'; 'A 1/6 probability of winning predicts 50 winners out of 300 lottery tickets.' Expected value is theoretical; actual experimental outcomes vary, with variability decreasing as n increases (law of large numbers).

questions.prompt: Expected number of heads in 100 coin flips.
questions.explanation: Expected value = probability × n = 1/2 × 100 = 50. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 tests expected value computation. Worked variations: expected tails = 50; expected heads in 200 flips = 100; expected tails in 50 flips = 25. State-test items test expected-value reasoning for coins, dice, and spinners. Actual experimental results approach the expected value as trials increase; small experiments (10 flips) may show large deviations (e.g., 7 heads in 10 flips), while large experiments (10,000 flips) converge to the expected value (about 5,000 heads). The probability and the number of trials interact in predictable ways via the expected-value formula.

questions.prompt: Expected number of 1s in 60 rolls of a fair die.
questions.explanation: Expected value = (1/6) × 60 = 10. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 tests expected value for dice. Worked variations: expected 6s in 60 rolls = 10; expected even numbers in 60 rolls = (1/2) × 60 = 30; expected numbers less than 4 in 60 rolls = (1/2) × 60 = 30. State-test items test expected-value calculations for various probability values: 'A spinner with 1/4 probability of red. In 80 spins, expected reds = (1/4) × 80 = 20.' Always multiply probability by trials to get expected count. Actual experimental results vary around the expected value; very large experiments minimize variability and approximate expected values closely.

questions.prompt: The principle that more trials give experimental probability closer to theoretical.
questions.explanation: The law of large numbers states that as the number of trials increases, the experimental probability of an event approaches the theoretical probability. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 implicitly addresses this in connecting experimental and theoretical models. Worked example: flip a fair coin 10 times, you might get 7 heads (P = 0.7); flip 1,000 times, you'll get approximately 500 heads (P ≈ 0.5); flip 1,000,000 times, very close to 500,000 heads (P → 0.5). State-test items test law-of-large-numbers reasoning: 'After many trials, the experimental probability should be close to the theoretical value'; 'A casino's house edge ensures long-run profit even though individual games may go either way.' LLN is the bridge between probability theory and statistical observation.

questions.prompt: Predicting outcomes of a real-world chance process.
questions.explanation: Prediction uses probability models to forecast real-world chance outcomes. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7 connects to real-world prediction. Worked example: a weather model predicting 70% chance of rain means in 100 similar weather situations, rain occurs in about 70 of them; predicting next month's rain on individual days requires probability reasoning. State-test items test multi-step prediction: 'A coffee shop has 60% probability of selling at least 20 lattes per day. Over a 30-day month, on how many days is it expected to sell at least 20?' Expected days = 0.60 × 30 = 18. Prediction problems combine probability with arithmetic and reasoning—frequent state-test item types that integrate Unit 6 with broader real-world reasoning.

questions.prompt: The constant ratio in y = kx.
questions.explanation: The constant of proportionality k = y/x in a proportional relationship y = kx is the unit rate (the y-value when x = 1) and the slope of the line through the origin. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.RP.2.B explicitly tests identifying k. Worked example: if 4 movie tickets cost $48, then k = 48/4 = $12 per ticket. The equation y = 12x predicts cost for any number of tickets. State-test items test k identification from tables, graphs, equations, and word problems. The unit rate is the most important number in a proportional relationship—it lets you compute any pairing of values quickly.

questions.prompt: Decimal form of 30%.
questions.explanation: To convert percent to decimal, divide by 100 or move decimal two places left: 30% → 0.3. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.RP.3 tests percent-decimal conversion for solving multistep problems. Common Grade 7 conversions: 25% = 0.25 = 1/4; 50% = 0.5 = 1/2; 75% = 0.75 = 3/4; 30% = 0.3 = 3/10. Worked example: 30% of 80 = 0.3 × 80 = 24. State-test items test rapid percent-decimal conversion in tax, tip, discount, and markup problems where students multiply the original price by the decimal form. Conversion fluency speeds all percent calculations.

questions.prompt: Tax on a $40 item at 8%.
questions.explanation: Tax = (tax rate as decimal) × price = 0.08 × 40 = $3.20. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.RP.3 explicitly tests tax problems. Total cost = $40 + $3.20 = $43.20, or compute total directly: $40 × 1.08 = $43.20. State-test items test multi-step tax problems combining tax with discount, tip, or other percent operations. Common conversion: 8% = 0.08 (move decimal two places left, or divide by 100); 7.5% = 0.075; 12% = 0.12. Always verify the calculation: $3.20 is 8/100 of $40, equivalent to 4/50, or 2/25—simplifies to checking decimal place value.

questions.prompt: Discount on a $60 item at 20% off.
questions.explanation: Discount = (discount rate) × original price = 0.20 × 60 = $12. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.RP.3 tests discount problems. Sale price = $60 − $12 = $48, or compute directly: $60 × 0.80 = $48 (since 100% − 20% = 80%). State-test items test multi-step discount problems combined with tax: 'Original price $60, 20% off, then 8% tax: sale price = $48, tax = $3.84, final = $51.84.' Standard order: discount first (reduce price), then tax on the discounted price. Always check whether the question asks for discount amount ($12), sale price ($48), or final price after tax ($51.84).

questions.prompt: Percent change from $50 to $60.
questions.explanation: Percent change = (new − old)/old × 100 = (60 − 50)/50 × 100 = 10/50 × 100 = 20%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.RP.3 tests percent change. The 20% is an increase because new > old; if old > new, it's a decrease. Worked variations: $80 to $60 is (60 − 80)/80 × 100 = −25% (a 25% decrease); $40 to $50 is +25% (a 25% increase). State-test items test both percent increase and decrease in real contexts (population, sales, prices). Common error: dividing by the new value instead of the old; always divide by the ORIGINAL (starting) value to compute the rate of change.

questions.prompt: The result of -8 + 5.
questions.explanation: To add integers with different signs: subtract absolute values, keep sign of larger absolute value. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.NS.1 tests integer addition with mixed signs. Worked example: −8 + 5: |−8| − |5| = 8 − 5 = 3; |−8| > |5|, so keep negative: answer = −3. Visualize on number line: start at −8, move 5 units right, land at −3. State-test items test integer operations in real contexts: temperature change, account balance, elevation. Practice mental math: '−8 plus 5' = 'subtract 5 from 8, then negate' = −3. Sign rule for addition: same signs add, different signs subtract; sign of larger absolute value dominates.

questions.prompt: The result of 2/3 + 1/4.
questions.explanation: To add fractions: find common denominator, convert, add numerators, simplify. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.NS.1 extends to rational fractions. Worked example: 2/3 + 1/4: LCD = 12; 2/3 = 8/12, 1/4 = 3/12; sum = 8/12 + 3/12 = 11/12. State-test items test fraction operations in mixed contexts: '1/2 cup flour + 1/3 cup sugar = 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6 cup total.' Always find LCD first; multiply numerator and denominator by the same factor to maintain equality. Simplify the result if possible: 11/12 is already in simplest form (GCF = 1). Common error: adding numerators AND denominators directly (2/3 + 1/4 ≠ 3/7); must find common denominator.

questions.prompt: The result of -4 × 6.
questions.explanation: For multiplying integers: different signs give negative product. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.NS.2 tests integer multiplication. Worked example: −4 × 6: different signs (negative × positive), |−4| × |6| = 24, apply negative: −24. Sign rules: (−)(−) = (+); (+)(+) = (+); (−)(+) = (−); (+)(−) = (−). Same rules apply to division. State-test items test integer operations extensively. Practice: −5 × −3 = +15; 7 × −4 = −28; −12 ÷ 4 = −3; −24 ÷ −6 = +4. The 'same signs positive, different signs negative' rule applies to both × and ÷.

questions.prompt: The result of 0.6 ÷ 0.2.
questions.explanation: To divide decimals: multiply both divisor and dividend by power of 10 to make divisor a whole number, then divide. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.NS.2.B tests decimal division. Worked example: 0.6 ÷ 0.2: multiply both by 10 → 6 ÷ 2 = 3. State-test items test decimal division in unit-rate contexts: 'A 2.5-pound bag of rice costs $4.75; unit price = $4.75 / 2.5 = (multiply both by 10) $47.50 / 25 = $1.90/lb.' Always clear decimals first to simplify division. Alternative method: long division with decimals, aligning carefully; multiplying by 10 is easier and faster. Common error: trying to align decimal points (which is the rule for ADDITION, not division).

questions.prompt: The decimal form of 3/8.
questions.explanation: To convert fraction to decimal: divide numerator by denominator. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.NS.2.D explicitly tests fraction-to-decimal conversion. Worked example: 3/8 = 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375 (3.000 ÷ 8 = 0.375, terminating decimal). State-test items test fraction-decimal conversion in mixed problems. Common fractions: 1/8 = 0.125; 3/8 = 0.375; 5/8 = 0.625; 7/8 = 0.875; 1/4 = 0.25; 3/4 = 0.75; 1/3 = 0.333...; 2/3 = 0.667. Fractions with denominators having only 2 and 5 as factors give terminating decimals; other denominators give repeating decimals. Practice conversion to speed mixed-form computation on the state test.

questions.prompt: The value of x in 2x + 3 = 11.
questions.explanation: To solve 2x + 3 = 11: subtract 3 → 2x = 8; divide by 2 → x = 4. Check: 2(4) + 3 = 11 ✓. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.EE.4.A explicitly tests two-step equations. Worked variations: 3x − 5 = 16 → x = 7; 4x + 2 = 18 → x = 4; (x+1)/3 = 4 → x = 11. State-test items test multiple two-step formats; the pattern is (1) undo addition/subtraction; (2) undo multiplication/division. Common error: doing operations in wrong order (e.g., dividing first); always reverse PEMDAS. Two-step equations are the foundational pre-algebra skill—mastery predicts success in Grade 8 algebra.

questions.prompt: The solution to 2x + 5 < 11.
questions.explanation: To solve 2x + 5 < 11: subtract 5 → 2x < 6; divide by 2 → x < 3. Inequality stays same (no negative). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.EE.4.B tests two-step inequalities. Worked variations: 3x − 7 > 8 → x > 5; (x − 1)/4 ≤ 2 → x ≤ 9. State-test items test two-step inequality solving with the same procedure as equations, but with sign-direction awareness. Graph x < 3: open circle at 3, shading left. Solution represents infinitely many values (2, 1, 0, −1, etc.). Critical rule: dividing both sides by a NEGATIVE flips the inequality sign; this is the most-tested concept.

questions.prompt: The result of expanding 3(2x - 5).
questions.explanation: Apply the distributive property: 3(2x − 5) = 3 × 2x + 3 × (−5) = 6x − 15. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.EE.1 explicitly tests distribution. Worked variations: 4(x + 3) = 4x + 12; −2(x + 4) = −2x − 8 (negative distributes to both terms); 1/2 (4x + 6) = 2x + 3. State-test items test the distributive property with positive and negative multipliers, fractions, and decimals. Common error: forgetting to distribute to the second term, or losing signs on negative distribution. Always multiply outside number by EVERY term inside parentheses with appropriate signs.

questions.prompt: The result of factoring 8x + 12.
questions.explanation: To factor: find GCF of all terms, divide each term by GCF, write as GCF × remaining. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.EE.1 explicitly tests factoring (reverse of distribution). Worked example: 8x + 12: GCF(8, 12) = 4; divide each term by 4: 8x/4 = 2x; 12/4 = 3; factored: 4(2x + 3). Check by expanding: 4(2x + 3) = 8x + 12 ✓. State-test items test linear factoring with positive coefficients (most common) and mixed signs. Factoring is the inverse of distribution; both operations are essential algebraic skills that appear throughout Grade 7 and prepare students for full algebra in Grade 8.

questions.prompt: The equation 'twice a number plus 5 equals 17.'
questions.explanation: To translate: 'twice a number' = 2x; 'plus 5' = +5; 'equals 17' = =17. Equation: 2x + 5 = 17, solving to x = 6. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.EE.4.A explicitly tests writing equations from word problems. State-test items present rich contexts: 'The total cost is $5 plus $3 per hour. For $20: 5 + 3h = 20 → h = 5 hours.' Translation keywords: 'sum/plus/more than/increased by' = +; 'difference/less than/decreased by' = −; 'product/times/of/twice/triple' = ×; 'quotient/divided by/per' = ÷; 'is/equals/results in' = =. Always identify variable, operations, and equality before writing.

questions.prompt: The area of a circle with radius 4.
questions.explanation: To find area: A = πr² = π × 4² = 16π. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.G.4 tests circle area. As decimal: 16 × 3.14 = 50.24 (or 16 × 3.14159 ≈ 50.27). State-test items often allow exact form (16π) or decimal approximation. Worked variations: A circle with radius 5: A = 25π ≈ 78.5; radius 10: A = 100π ≈ 314. Always use r² (square the radius), not 2r (which is the diameter). Common error: confusing area and circumference formulas; remember area uses r²; circumference uses r (with factor 2π) or d (with factor π). Practice both formulas with the same circle for clarity.

questions.prompt: The sum of interior angles of a triangle.
questions.explanation: The interior angles of any triangle sum to 180°. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.G.5 tests angle problems including triangles. Worked example: if a triangle has angles 50° and 75°, the third angle = 180 − 50 − 75 = 55°. State-test items test missing-angle problems frequently. Right triangles have one 90° angle, so the other two are complementary (sum to 90°). Equilateral triangles have all three angles = 60°. Practice: 'In a triangle, two angles are equal. The third is 80°. Each equal angle = (180 − 80)/2 = 50°.' Triangle angle sum is foundational to all triangle geometry.

questions.prompt: Two angles whose measures sum to 90 degrees.
questions.explanation: Complementary angles sum to 90°—together they form a right angle. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.G.5 explicitly tests complementary angles in multi-step problems. Worked example: if one angle is 35°, its complement is 90 − 35 = 55°. State-test items test computing complements and setting up equations: 'If two complementary angles have x and 2x as measures, x + 2x = 90 → 3x = 90 → x = 30°, 2x = 60°.' Right angles in figures (where two perpendicular lines meet, or in right triangles' acute angles) typically signal complementary pairs.

questions.prompt: Surface area of a cube with side 5.
questions.explanation: To find SA: 6s² = 6 × 5² = 6 × 25 = 150 sq units. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.G.6 tests surface area of cubes. A cube has 6 congruent faces, each s²; total SA = 6s². Worked variations: side 3: SA = 6 × 9 = 54; side 10: SA = 600. State-test items test surface area in packaging contexts. Distinguish surface area (sq units, 2D coverage) from volume (cubic units, 3D capacity). For a cube of side 5: SA = 150 (paint coverage); V = 125 (water capacity)—two different concepts measuring two different attributes. Always check whether the problem asks for SA or V.

questions.prompt: Volume of a rectangular prism with dimensions 3 × 4 × 5.
questions.explanation: To find volume: V = LWH = 3 × 4 × 5 = 60 cubic units. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.G.6 tests volume of rectangular prisms. State-test items test volume in real contexts: 'A box 8 cm × 5 cm × 3 cm has volume 120 cm³'; 'A swimming pool 25 m × 10 m × 1.5 m has volume 375 m³.' Units always cubed: cm³, m³, in³, ft³. Distinguish from surface area (sq units). For irregular shapes, decompose into rectangular prisms or other simple shapes, compute each volume, and sum. Volume problems often combine with rate problems on the state test (e.g., fill a tank at a given flow rate).

questions.prompt: P(heads) on a fair coin.
questions.explanation: On a fair coin, P(heads) = 1/2 = 0.5 = 50%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.5 and 7.SP.7 test simple probability. Worked example: P(tails) = 1/2 also; P(heads or tails) = 1 (certain); P(neither) = 0 (impossible). State-test items test fair-coin probability as the simplest probability example, foundational for compound events with multiple flips (P(HH) = 1/4; P(HHH) = 1/8). The 'fair' assumption uses the uniform probability model—each outcome equally likely. For unfair (biased) coins, experimental probability based on observed frequencies replaces the theoretical 1/2.

questions.prompt: P(rolling a 4) on a fair die.
questions.explanation: On a fair die, P(rolling 4) = favorable/total = 1/6 ≈ 0.167 ≈ 16.7%. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.7.A tests theoretical probability with equally likely outcomes. Worked variations: P(rolling even) = 3/6 = 1/2; P(rolling ≥ 4) = 3/6 = 1/2; P(rolling less than 7) = 1 (certain). State-test items test fair-die probability for various event compositions; always count favorable outcomes (here: just 4) and divide by total (6). Connect to compound events: P(rolling 4 twice) = 1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36 (independent events use multiplication rule).

questions.prompt: The mean of 70, 80, 90.
questions.explanation: To find mean: sum / count = (70 + 80 + 90) / 3 = 240/3 = 80. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.3 tests mean as measure of center. Worked variations: mean of 60, 70, 80 = 70; mean of 70, 80, 90, 100 = 85. State-test items test mean computation in comparative inference. Mean is sensitive to outliers: mean of 70, 80, 90, 1000 = 310 (the outlier pulls mean up dramatically). For symmetric distributions, mean equals median; for skewed distributions, mean differs from median in the direction of the skew. Mean is the most common measure of center on the state test.

questions.prompt: A sample where every member has equal chance of selection.
questions.explanation: A random sample has every population member equally likely to be selected—the gold standard for valid inference. NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.1 explicitly tests random sampling. Worked example: drawing names from a hat, using a random number generator. State-test items test random-sample identification and contrast with biased samples: 'surveying only honor-roll students' is biased (not random); 'randomly drawing 50 names from the student database' is random. Random sampling enables generalization from sample to population with controlled uncertainty—the foundational concept of statistical inference. Convenience samples and voluntary-response samples are NOT random.

questions.prompt: P(rolling a 3 then a 5) on two independent dice.
questions.explanation: For independent events: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B). NYS Grade 7 Math standard 7.SP.8.A tests compound probability with independent events. Worked example: P(roll 3) × P(roll 5) = 1/6 × 1/6 = 1/36. State-test items test compound probability for dice, coins, spinners, and card draws. Practice: P(HHH) for three flips = 1/2 × 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/8; P(rolling doubles) on two dice = 6/36 = 1/6 (six possible doubles out of 36 total outcomes). Always check independence: with replacement = independent; without replacement = dependent (probabilities change). Grade 7 typically tests independent events; conditional/dependent probability is Grade 8+.
